region_of_origin: India (North Indian / Mughlai tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic kebab class; no specific Hara Bhara Kebab entry exists. Closest defensible parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Hara Bhara Kebab code. A0CKP (vegetable-based patties/cutlets) is the closest defensible category given the spinach-pea-potato composition.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Hara Bhara Kebab. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual Indian dish preparations at this level of specificity.

allergens: gluten (chickpea flour binder may vary; wheat flour sometimes used)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Hara Bhara Kebab consists of 2 pieces weighing approximately 100 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Hara Bhara Kebab — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hara-Bhara Kebab (Chef Recommendation), cr_id G00010871, with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated at 3.5 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for shallow-fried vegetable patties with similar fat content (USDA SR Legacy #11457 spinach-based preparation analogy). Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat not reported; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on potato-and-vegetable patty composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hara Bhara Kebab. GI estimated at 55 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for potato-vegetable patties and mixed vegetable cutlets. The dominant starch source is potato (GI ~70 boiled), moderated substantially by the high fat content (26.4 g/100g), protein (10.7 g/100g), spinach, and green peas, which slow gastric emptying and reduce glycaemic response. Composite GI of 55 is a conservative medium estimate consistent with analogous fried vegetable patty entries. GL per 100 g serving = (55 × 5.24) / 100 = 2.9 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — critically elevated, far exceeding the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Hara Bhara Kebab due to its critically high sodium content of 1080 mg/100g; if consumed, restrict to a single small piece and request a significantly reduced-salt preparation, avoiding pairing with high-sodium chutneys or sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) with medium estimated GI of 55, but very high fat (26.4 g/100g) raises insulin resistance concern
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (5.24 g/100g) and medium GI of approximately 55 make Hara Bhara Kebab a relatively low glycaemic-load option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, but the high fat content (26.4 g/100g) and elevated sodium (1080 mg/100g) warrant portion control; request grilled rather than shallow-fried preparation and avoid pairing with naan or white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100g), critically high sodium (1080 mg/100g), and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Hara Bhara Kebab as an occasional food only; the combination of high fat (26.4 g/100g), high sodium (1080 mg/100g), and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) is unfavourable for heart health, and grilled preparation with significantly reduced salt is strongly recommended if consumed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA gate; shallow-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs; potatoes are nightshade vegetables
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Hara Bhara Kebab in its standard form due to critically high sodium (1080 mg/100g), potential AGE formation from shallow-frying, and the presence of nightshade vegetables (potato); grilled preparation with substantially reduced salt is the only conditionally acceptable modification.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hara Bhara Kebab is a popular North Indian vegetarian kebab whose name translates literally as 'green and lush kebab' in Hindi. It is made from a mixture of blanched spinach, boiled green peas, and mashed potato, seasoned with mint, fresh coriander, green chilli, garam masala, cumin, and other Indian spices, then shaped into flat round or oval patties and shallow-fried or grilled until golden and crisp on the outside. The texture is soft and moist inside with a lightly crisp exterior; the flavour is herbaceous, mildly spiced, and earthy. In Singapore, Hara Bhara Kebab appears on Indian restaurant menus as a starter or snack, often served with mint chutney or tamarind sauce. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore Foodo database records 302 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 26.4 g fat, and 5.24 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with a notably high sodium content of 1080 mg/100g. The high fat reflects shallow-frying oil absorption. The low carbohydrate content gives a low glycaemic load despite a medium estimated GI of approximately 55. Clinically, the critically high sodium makes this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis without significant recipe modification. The high fat and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) also raise cardiovascular concern. Spinach and green peas contribute beneficial micronutrients including calcium (118 mg/100g), iron (1.15 mg/100g), and potassium (387 mg/100g). This dish is suitable for vegetarians and is generally halal-compliant.

Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous shallow-fried vegetable patty data and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for potato-vegetable patties, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean-served Indian dish are present.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this specific preparation; this is an HPB-reported value and is retained as confirmed zero per the approved source, though it is nutritionally atypical for a spinach-pea-potato mixture — likely reflects the specific chef preparation tested. 2. saturated_fat_g set to 3.5 g/100g: not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous shallow-fried vegetable patty entry (closest approved-source equivalent); documented as adapted. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific HPB Hara Bhara Kebab preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Hara Bhara Kebab was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 5. Cholesterol at 85 mg/100g is noted from HPB; this is unusually high for a vegetarian dish and may reflect egg or dairy binder used in the chef preparation. 6. is_vegan set to false because the chef preparation likely uses egg or paneer as a binder given the cholesterol value of 85 mg/100g.
